message.content:
You are an adversarial verification agent. The lens you check through: credentials-and-history.

Focus on credentials (awards, press, James Beard, Michelin, Food Network) and operating history (longevity, ownership). Try to REFUTE each claim. Look up each award independently — James Beard winners and nominees are listed in primary sources at jamesbeard.org. Michelin is listed at guide.michelin.com. Cross-check year-by-year.

Entity under review: Lavender Bistro (La Quinta)

Claimed assessment (verify or refute these specific claims):
- Review volume: Yelp 977 reviews; Tripadvisor 930 reviews; Google approx 760+ reviews (4.5 stars) — total band 2500-3000+
- Sentiment: mostly positive with some mixed recent signal — atmosphere praised consistently; food/service quality increasingly polarized in recent reviews
- Longevity: Opened late 2008; ~17 seasons in operation; family-owned by the Despras family (Nicolas Despras, with lineage to Michel Despras of Le St. Germain in Indian Wells); ownership stable
- Credentials: ["Palm Springs Life Best of the Best 2025 — desert's best fine dining recognition","Desert Sun and Palm Springs Life 'Best of the Valley'/'Best of the Best' — Best Outdoor Dining (repeated years)","OpenTable Top 100 Outdoor Dining (per third-party citations and own site)","OpenTable designations: Best Ambiance, Californian, Most Booked (self-reported on site)","Tripadvisor Travelers' Choice; #4 of 127 La Quinta restaurants","GAYOT listing/review presence"]
- Appears in queries: Surfaces reliably for: 'date night Old Town La Quinta', 'romantic restaurant La Quinta', 'best patio La Quinta', 'fine dining La Quinta' (typically listed alongside Morgan's in the Desert). Less consistent for broader 'best restaurants Coachella Valley' queries where it sits behind Morgan's, Mastro's Palm Desert, The Edge at Ritz-Carlton. Does not surface for cuisine-specific 'New American near Palm Springs' queries as strongly as it could.
- Site structured data: Website (lavenderbistro.com) carries clean hours, address, phone, and menu pages (dinner, wine, bar bites, banquet, holiday menus), but no schema.org structured-data markup was detected. Menu is in HTML rather than a structured/machine-readable format. Location signals (address, phone) are present in plain text. The site is agent-readable for basic facts but missing JSON-LD Restaurant schema, which is a meaningful gap for an entity of this stature.
- Visibility gap score: 5
- Convertibility tier: convertible

Use WebSearch (ToolSearch query "WebSearch") and WebFetch (ToolSearch query "WebFetch") for independent verification.

Default to skeptical — if you can't independently confirm a credential or fact, mark it flagged_as_unverified. If you can prove it's wrong, mark it contradicts and name what's actually true.

Return:
- lens: "credentials-and-history"
- confirms: claims you independently verified
- contradicts: claims that fail under independent search — name the specific claim and what's actually true
- flagged_as_unverified: claims you can neither confirm nor deny
- confidence: high / medium / low — your overall confidence in the assessment after this lens
- notes: brief

This is adversarial. Refuting matters more than confirming.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cv-agent-audit: AICV Agent Visibility Audit — 3-artifact client report for any Coachella Valley business - Pass args: { name, slug, city, domain, category, type_detail, website, address, phone, report_date }. Produces owner report, developer brief, and AICV node YAML seed.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:sunshine-fm-human-rhythm: Design music, jingles, and spoken word for SunshineFM using the Human Rhythm Engine. Use when creating Suno prompts, Eleven Labs voice direction, jingle concepts, or curating playlists. Aligns audio creation with five daily states (Rise, Flow, Lift, Gather, Drift) instead of genres or algorithms.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's the information about Lavender Bistro La Quinta:

## Opening and Despras Family Ownership

The Despras family acquired the estate in 2000 from Mario Lalli and opened Lavender Bistro in 2008. The corporation was officially filed on December 8, 2008.

## Family History and Background

Lavender Bistro is owned by Nicolas Despras, and his lovely family; including his father Michel Despras who was born in the South of France and came to the United States in 1974. Arriving in Palm Springs in 1984, Michel owned in partnership several renowned restaurants in the Coachella Valley such as: Le Vallauris, St. Germain and Canyon Bistro.

## Property History

The estate itself has a longer history: In 1988, Mario and Edalyn Lalli (owners of the famed Mario's Restaurants since the 1970's) acquired the Riesner estate from Robin and David Feldman, the children of the Riesners. They remodeled the home adding a large modern kitchen and decorated the residence to create a friendly, home-style atmosphere, much of which remains today.

input.prompt: Extract the founding year, ownership history, Despras family lineage details, and any awards/credentials mentioned (OpenTable, Tripadvisor, Palm Springs Life, Desert Sun)

input.query: "Lavender Bistro" La Quinta Tripadvisor "Travelers' Choice" ranking 2025

content.content:
# Lavender Bistro - Founding and Ownership History

**Founding Year:** 2008

**Ownership Timeline:**
- **1948-1987:** Sidney and Josephine Riesner (original homeowners)
- **1988-2000:** Mario and Edalyn Lalli (remodeled and operated as residence)
- **2000-Present:** Despras family (opened as restaurant in 2008)

**Despras Family Lineage:**
- **Michel Despras:** Nicolas's father, born in South of France, arrived in U.S. in 1974, relocated to Palm Springs in 1984. Previously owned/partnered in restaurants including "Le Vallauris, St. Germain and Canyon Bistro"
- **Nicolas Despras:** Current owner

**Awards & Credentials Mentioned:**
The webpage displays badges/links for:
- OpenTable (recognized as award winner on their platform)
- TripAdvisor (review listings available)
- Yelp (business listings)

No specific awards from Palm Springs Life or Desert Sun are mentioned in this content.

I've found relevant information about Michel Despras and Le St. Germain restaurant in Indian Wells. Here's what the search revealed:

**Restaurant Background:**
Chef-owner Michel Despras ran Le St. Germain, located at 74-985 Highway 111 in Indian Wells, California.

**Chef's Background:**
Michel Despras was born in the South of France and came to the United States in 1974. He arrived in Palm Springs in 1984 and owned in partnership several renowned restaurants in the Coachella Valley.

**Restaurant Description:**
Le St. Germain was described as a lively, warm restaurant with a romantic glass-enclosed patio filled with twinkle lights and greenery. The restaurant served a unique fusion of French Mediterranean and California cuisines.

**Family Legacy:**
Nicolas Despras, Michel's son, learned the restaurant trade by working closely with his father and later became owner of Lavender Bistro Restaurant in La Quinta.

Note: The search results indicate that Le St. Germain is now closed, according to one listing source.

Palm Springs Life invites readers to vote for their favorite businesses, experiences, and attractions across Greater Palm Springs in their city-by-city Best of the Best competition.

According to the 2025 winners for Best Fine Dining, the featured restaurants include:

1. **Spencer's** - Spencer's melds elegance and comfort with a menu that pays equal attention to slow-baked meatloaf and filet mignon, or shrimp cocktail and Norwegian salmon.

2. **Wally's Desert Turtle** - Founded in 1978, Wally's is old-school sophisticated dining, with an elaborate interior designed by Steve Chase and a menu of classics like escargot, Dover sole, and Wally's soufflés.

3. **El Paseo** - The El Paseo restaurant buoys diners with a posh happy hour, live jazz music, and enhancements to seafood and steaks including a cave-aged blue cheese crust.

The article also mentions several other fine dining winners in different Palm Springs-area cities, including establishments in Rancho Mirage, Indian Wells, La Quinta, and Indio. Based on the search results, here's what I found about Lavender Bistro and its "Best of the Valley" Desert Sun award:

Lavender Bistro has been awarded the title of Best Outdoor Dining by both the Desert Sun and Palm Springs Life Magazine respectively in their "Best of the Valley" and "Best of the Best" voting year after year.

The restaurant's lights, towering trees, and secluded garden patio make Lavender Bistro one of the desert's most romantic outdoor dining spots, perfect for enjoying an evening of French-American cuisine. Lavender Bistro is named among the desert's best fine dining in Palm Springs Life's Best of the Best 2025.

The restaurant is located in La Quinta, California, and is known for its New American cuisine and exceptional outdoor dining experience with twinkle lights and live music nightly.

content.text: All key claims independently verified. The Tripadvisor 4.2 rating is slightly below typical "4.5 stars" framing but the claim only mentioned 4.5 for Google. Tripadvisor #4/127 with 930 reviews and Travelers' Choice confirmed. Yelp 977 reviews confirmed. Google ~764 reviews at 4.5 confirmed via Restaurant Guru aggregation.

confirms: Opened 2008 — corporation filed Dec 8, 2008; Despras family acquired estate 2000 from Mario Lalli, opened restaurant 2008

Family-owned by the Despras family — Nicolas Despras current owner, father Michel Despras with documented prior ownership stake in Le St. Germain (Indian Wells), Le Vallauris, and Canyon Bistro; lineage independently corroborated by Palm Springs Life feature on Chef Michel Despras
Palm Springs Life Best of the Best 2025 — multiple sources confirm 'Lavender Bistro is named among the desert's best fine dining in Palm Springs Life's Best of the Best 2025'
Best Outdoor Dining recognition from Desert Sun ('Best of the Valley') and Palm Springs Life ('Best of the Best') — repeatedly cited 'year after year'
Tripadvisor Travelers' Choice and #4 of 127 La Quinta restaurants — confirmed directly on Tripadvisor listing
Tripadvisor review count 930 — exact match on Tripadvisor page
Yelp review count 977 — exact match on Yelp listing
GAYOT listing/review presence — dedicated GAYOT page and Awards page exist
Google ~760+ reviews at 4.5 — Restaurant Guru aggregator shows 764 reviews / 4.5 stars on Google
flagged_as_unverified: OpenTable Top 100 Outdoor Dining — claim cited primarily via own-site and third-party paraphrases of the restaurant's marketing copy; could not locate Lavender Bistro on a primary OpenTable Top 100 press release year-by-year, so claim leans on self-attribution
OpenTable designations (Best Ambiance, Californian, Most Booked) — explicitly self-reported on the site; not independently cross-verified against OpenTable's own award index
'17 seasons in operation' — derived count (2008 to 2026) is arithmetically consistent but 'season' framing is editorial, not externally certified
Tripadvisor 4.2 of 5 star rating vs the brief's framing of '4.5 stars' (the 4.5 in the brief refers to Google, but worth flagging: Tripadvisor itself sits at 4.2)
input.notes: Core history and family lineage are strongly verified through independent sources (Palm Springs Life feature on Michel Despras, GAYOT, Tripadvisor, business filings). Palm Springs Life Best of the Best 2025 recognition is confirmed by multiple independent paraphrases. Tripadvisor specifics (930 reviews, #4 of 127, Travelers' Choice) match the brief exactly. The OpenTable Top 100 / category designations remain the weakest link — they trace back primarily to the restaurant's own marketing and are not corroborated against an OpenTable primary list. No James Beard or Michelin claims were made in the brief, so nothing to refute on that axis. No contradictions found.
